title: Into The Corridor
html:
The footsteps reach his room in roughly 2 minutes. Multiple sources: he counts 3 distinct rhythms spreading through the corridor wall, each with the doubled cadence from the nurse-figure earlier, but these are not moving as a single unit. One set continues west past his room without slowing. A second comes to a stop somewhere in the mid-corridor, 10 or 15 meters off by the acoustic weight of it. The third stays back near the east wing end of the building and settles into the same measured rest-pace they all default to when not traveling. He counts to 60 after the last movement stops. Nothing comes to his door.

At 7:52 the ventilation grille produces the Drag sound twice. He reads item 6 in his head without looking up. By 8:01 the corridor has been quiet for 17 uninterrupted minutes and he decides it is time to look at it.

He opens the door to approximately a half-inch. The corridor runs long in both directions, its walls the same institutional green with the same cracked plaster at the corners. The ceiling lights run at uneven intervals, most of them on, one dark at the far western end. From this angle he can make out 4 doors: the nearest 2 have red tape on their handles, single strips applied in an X across the hardware and extending onto the door surface. Further east, a door with no tape sits slightly ajar. Past it, roughly 30 meters from where he stands, a painted line crosses the full width of the corridor floor. The light on the far side of that line is the same fixture type as his end but with a gray cast rather than yellow. He checks his own doorframe and finds a small label mounted above it: 7-C.

'East wing starts at that line,' he thinks.

From somewhere past the painted boundary: a voice, male, speaking in short units. Three words, a pause, 2 or 3 words, a longer pause. The cadence is controlled, the tone of someone delivering information to themselves rather than reaching for anyone else's attention. It runs for roughly 15 seconds and cuts off cleanly.

Someone over there has a plan. He adds this to the things he has.

[This guy hasn't moved in almost 20 minutes]

[He's just standing in a crack in the door. Is he even breathing]

[The door has been open a half inch for 4 minutes now. Four. Minutes.]

He opens the door the rest of the way and steps into the corridor.

[HE'S OUT]

[okay so he IS capable of moving]

[He's not running. He's not crouching. He's just walking like it's a hallway at work]

[Person above, has it occurred to you that he might know something we don't]

[What does he know that makes walking out there okay???]

The smell is stronger outside the room: the cleaning chemical and the thing underneath it, both more present in the open air, and a third element he hadn't detected through the door gap, a low sustained sound from the east wing direction sitting just below what the ears register as a sound versus a pressure. It isn't the ventilation. It maintains itself at a steady level, irregular only at the very edges of each cycle, like something that breathes.

He goes to the nearest red-tape door. The tape is a single strip in an X across the handle, continuing up onto the door surface. Underneath one arm of the X, barely visible through the translucent tape, someone has written in ballpoint directly on the door. The writing is small and he has to get within 20 cm to read it. The first line says Room 7-D. The second says DO NOT. The third line runs beneath the tape's edge and the letters are not legible from here. He straightens up and looks east along the corridor toward the painted line.

Two white-uniform figures come around the far end of the corridor past the painted boundary, heading west. The stride length is consistent with walking and the posture is plausible, but the rate at which they cover ground is wrong. They cross 3 meters in the time a walking stride should take for 1, the gait correct at the joint level and wrong at the physics level, the way a video looks when the playback rate has been changed without adjusting the subject's actual movement. They are approximately 28 meters away. He steps back through the door of 7-C and pulls it shut with his hand flat against the frame to keep the Latch! from sounding.

Through the gap under the door the smell thickens as both footstep sets approach. One continues west without slowing. The other stops outside 7-C and he can see the shadow of feet in the corridor light: placed too close together, toe spacing slightly wrong in a way that feet don't arrive at by accident. The weight shifts once, right to left, slow. He counts: 11 seconds, the smell heavy enough to taste at the back of his throat, and then at 14 seconds the feet move on and follow the first set west.

He releases a breath he hadn't made a conscious decision to hold, notes that breath-holding had a tactical value of zero in this context, and finds that his hand is still shaking because he hasn't let go of the door handle.

[The thing stopped right outside his door and his face just. Didn't do anything]

[He looked like he was categorizing it]

[For 14 seconds?? I was screaming for 14 seconds]

[Did anyone catch how fast they move? That's not a walk, what is that]

[I'm going to need someone to explain this player to me]

[I've watched S-rank players do worse in the first hour. Who IS this]

He checks the wall clock: 8:19. Roughly 65 minutes in the instance. He steps away from the door and catalogs what the last 18 minutes have added. Room 7-C is his starting room. Room 7-D directly across is red-taped with writing he can only partially read. The east wing begins at the painted line. Two staff figures moved from the east wing at an incorrect speed and one of them stopped outside his door for 14 seconds. The sustained low sound is present in the corridor but wasn't audible from inside 7-C. The human voice came from east of the painted line. He has no clearance condition yet and the front desk location from item 9 is still unresolved.

At 8:22, footsteps start from the east wing direction, heading west. The rhythm is human: the specific biological irregularity of biological walking, interval variation that no mechanical process produces. They are moving carefully, a pace chosen for quiet. When they reach his door and stop, he positions his hand near the handle without gripping it and waits through 5 seconds of silence before the knock arrives.

Three Knocks!, the spacing between them uneven: first to second about 1 second, second to third slightly faster, the third landing harder than the others. The natural asymmetry of a real hand on a door. The nurse-figure's knock earlier had been metronomic, all 3 identical. This is the other kind.

[Human knock or monster knock. Someone tell me which one this is]

[The earlier knock was like a metronome. That one isn't]

[Please answer the door. Please please please]

[I can't believe I'm invested in a guy who stood in a doorway for 4 minutes]

He stands at the door with the handle in reach. Item 3 says don't communicate with unresponsive patients. The person outside knocked and is waiting for an answer: responsive, by the exact language of the sign. He opens the door.

The person standing in the corridor is male, late 20s, wearing what used to be a clean set of civilian clothes before however many hours of this building got into them. The collar of his shirt is turned under on one side in a way he clearly hasn't noticed. He is holding a length of metal pipe at his side, his grip on it loose and practiced rather than tense, which means he picked it up a while ago and has been carrying it long enough for the weight to become familiar. His eyes go to Jiang Wenxu's face and stay there.

Neither of them says anything for 3 seconds.

"You're the one from 7-C," the man said. It isn't a question.

"Yes," Jiang Wenxu replied.

The man's eyes track briefly to the corridor behind Jiang Wenxu, then back. "How long have you been in there."

"About an hour and 20 minutes."

Something shifts in the man's expression, minor and quickly flattened. He glances at the door handle, the lack of tape, then back at the face. "Song Yu," he said, and did not offer a hand. "Third instance."

[THIRD INSTANCE veteran in here?? How is he not dead]

[The pipe. HE HAS A PIPE]

[The new player still doesn't look scared. Song Yu does though. A little]

[Third instance and he found a weapon and this other guy found NOTHING and he's looking at him like he's the threat here]

"Jiang Wenxu," he replied. "First."

Song Yu stared at him for a moment. "First instance," he repeated.

"Yes."

Song Yu looked at the door of 7-C, looked at the corridor in both directions, and looked back at Jiang Wenxu. He did not ask what he was obviously wanting to ask, which was how a first-instance player had just survived in one room without a weapon for 80 minutes while Song Yu had spent his first 90 minutes in this building hiding in a supply closet in the east wing. Instead he said, "There's an administrator's office at the end of the east corridor. Past the painted line, turn right, down 40 meters, last door on the left. That's where we need to go."

Jiang Wenxu thought of item 9. All visitors must sign out at the front desk before leaving. He thought of the exact word front desk and the exact word administrator's office and the gap between them.

"What makes you think that's the exit," he said.

Song Yu's jaw tightened slightly. "Because item 9 says you sign out to leave, and the only sign-out location in a facility like this is administration. I've cleared 2 instances before, I know how these things are structured."

"Item 9 says front desk," Jiang Wenxu said. "Not administrator's office."

"They're the same thing."

"Maybe." He was not confident they were. The rule used a specific word for a specific location, and in an instance where every other item had been precise enough to function as a death condition, the word choice was probably not interchangeable. But he didn't have enough information to be certain, and Song Yu had 40 meters of east wing navigation on him. "What's between here and there."

Song Yu's mouth pressed flat. "Three of the staff in the main east corridor. They don't move from their positions. There's a gap of about 8 meters between the second and third where the corridor bends, and if you move fast through it you can get past without them tracking you." He paused. "The second and third time I tried. First time I didn't make it to the bend."

"What happened the first time," Jiang Wenxu said.

"I ran," Song Yu said flatly.

A wrong thing to do, based on item 1. The noise and movement restriction kicked in after 10 PM, but the pattern of behavior the rules described was probably not exclusive to nighttime hours. He filed this without commenting on it.

From the west end of the corridor, where the light had been out since he first looked: a Creak! of weight on linoleum, slow and even. Both of them turned their heads. One white-uniform figure was stepping out from the darkened section, 35 meters away, its back to them. It stood in place and did not turn.

Song Yu's grip on the pipe went from loose to deliberate. "We need to move," he said quietly.

Jiang Wenxu looked at the figure, which had not turned and was not moving toward them. He looked at the painted line east of them. He thought of item 7, the specific phrasing of it, the thirty-second interval starting from the knock rather than from when the door opened. He thought of the nurse-figure that had held his door for 32 seconds and then walked away.

He didn't think moving was the right answer. But Song Yu had already taken two steps east and was looking back at him with an expression that didn't have room in it for a methodical discussion of door rules.

The survival probability notification had not moved. Still calculating, still arriving at nothing, positioned in the right corner of his vision like an administrative error that no one had gotten around to closing.
